cases.query: Why should RAG evaluation be split into layers?

expectedSources.heading: Intro
expectedSources.snippet: RAG evaluation decomposes into three layers: retrieval quality, generation quality, and end-to-end usefulness.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Evaluation should separate retrieval, generation, and end-to-end behavior so regressions are attributable.

cases.query: What retrieval metric should I track first for RAG?

expectedSources.heading: Retrieval Metrics
expectedSources.snippet: **Recall@k** — of all relevant documents in the corpus, what fraction appears in the top-k results. Recall@5 = 0.8 means 80% of relevant documents land in the top 5. This is the primary retrieval metric for RAG because the generator cannot use evidence it never sees. A recall failure is a hard ceiling on answer quality.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Track Recall@k first because missing evidence is a hard ceiling on answer quality.

cases.query: When is MRR useful in RAG evaluation?

expectedSources.heading: Retrieval Metrics
expectedSources.snippet: **MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)** — the average of 1/rank for the first relevant document across queries. If the first relevant result is at position 3, the reciprocal rank is 1/3. MRR rewards pushing the best result higher. Useful when the generator primarily uses the top-ranked chunk.

cases.query: What does high faithfulness with low context recall mean?

expectedSources.heading: Diagnostic Combinations
expectedSources.snippet: | High | Low | Retrieval ceiling — model uses what it gets correctly, but evidence is missing | Hybrid retrieval, expand k, fix metadata filters, improve embeddings |

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: This points to a retrieval ceiling, not a generation problem.

cases.query: What is the main risk of fixed-size chunking?

expectedSources.heading: Fixed-Size Chunking
expectedSources.snippet: - **Boundary cuts through logical units.** A policy clause, code block, or table row gets split at an arbitrary token offset. The retriever returns a fragment that looks relevant but is incomplete, and the model generates a confidently wrong answer. Mitigate by increasing overlap and auditing retrieval on structured documents.

cases.query: How does parent-child chunking improve answer completeness?

expectedSources.heading: Parent-Child Chunking
expectedSources.snippet: - At retrieval time, search against child chunks for precision — small chunks match specific queries better. When a child matches, expand to its parent chunk before passing context to the generator. The parent provides the surrounding context that the child alone may lack.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Retrieve small chunks for precision, then expand to parent context for synthesis.

cases.query: Why can vector search miss technical identifiers?

expectedSources.heading: Dense Retrieval -- Vector Search
expectedSources.snippet: - **Misses exact identifiers.** Embedding models learn to capture meaning, not specific tokens. Error codes, API paths, and version strings get lost in the vector representation. The retriever returns topically related but operationally wrong chunks — and unlike keyword search misses (which return obviously unrelated content), vector search misses look plausible, making them harder to catch.

cases.query: Should translated queries replace the original query?

expectedSources.heading: Pitfalls
expectedSources.snippet: Mitigation: include the original query as one of the retrieval variants — never translate only, always include the original. Set explicit constraints in the translation prompt ("preserve all specific entities, identifiers, and version numbers"). Evaluate translated queries against the original: if cosine similarity between a variant and the original drops below a threshold, discard the variant before retrieval.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Keep the original query in the candidate set so translations do not drift away from intent.

cases.query: Can reranking recover documents that retrieval missed?

expectedSources.heading: Pitfalls
expectedSources.snippet: Reranking can only reorder what retrieval found. If the relevant document is not in the candidate set at all (recall failure), no amount of reranking will surface it. Teams sometimes add rerankers expecting them to fix retrieval coverage problems, when the actual fix is better [[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/LLM/RAG/Chunking|chunking]], [[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/LLM/Embeddings|embedding model selection]], or query expansion.

cases.query: What happens when you swap embedding models?

expectedSources.heading: Pitfalls
expectedSources.snippet: Changing the embedding model — even a minor version — invalidates every stored vector. The new model produces vectors in a different geometric space. Cosine similarity between old and new vectors is meaningless.

cases.query: Why can a golden evaluation set go stale?

expectedSources.path: Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md
expectedSources.heading: Pitfalls
expectedSources.snippet: **What goes wrong**: the golden set was built from early user queries. Six months later, user behavior has shifted — new question types, new product features, new failure modes.

cases.query: Why do global monitoring metrics hide tenant regressions?

expectedSources.heading: Pitfalls
expectedSources.snippet: Global aggregate metrics hide localized regressions. A pipeline change that improves average faithfulness by 2% can simultaneously degrade faithfulness by 20% for a specific tenant whose documents use a different format.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Segment monitoring by tenant or query cluster so localized regressions are not averaged away.

cases.query: How can I evaluate whether a chunking strategy retrieves the exact evidence span instead of noisy context?

expectedSources.heading: Chunking Evaluation
expectedSources.snippet: Token IoU is more informative than end-to-end Recall@k because it captures chunk efficiency. A chunking strategy can achieve high retrieval recall by retrieving large, noisy chunks while wasting context window tokens on irrelevant content.

expectedSources.heading: Practical Baselines
expectedSources.snippet: Track per-source retrieval failure modes before switching strategy. Aggregate metrics hide format-specific problems — a retriever can perform well on prose while consistently breaking on table-heavy docs.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Use span-level/token-overlap metrics plus per-source failure analysis, not just document-level recall.

cases.query: A query contains an exact error code or API path; why might vector retrieval look plausible but still fail, and what retrieval mode helps?

expectedSources.heading: Dense Retrieval — Vector Search
expectedSources.snippet: Embedding models learn to capture meaning, not specific tokens. Error codes, API paths, and version strings get lost in the vector representation.

expectedSources.heading: Hybrid Retrieval — Vector + Keyword
expectedSources.snippet: Identifier-heavy corpora benefit from higher keyword weight (alpha around 0.4); conversational queries benefit from higher vector weight (alpha around 0.7).

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Vector search can miss exact identifiers; hybrid retrieval or stronger keyword weighting should cover those lexical constraints.

cases.query: When should I finetune embeddings instead of changing chunking or retrieval logic?

expectedSources.heading: Questions
expectedSources.snippet: Finetuning is justified when retrieval failures are specifically caused by semantic mismatches on domain terminology — queries and relevant documents contain the same domain concepts but land far apart in vector space.

expectedSources.heading: How to Choose a Strategy
expectedSources.snippet: Use retrieval failures, not preference, to pick a strategy.

cases.expectedAnswerSummary: Finetune only when failures are semantic/domain-term mismatches; fix chunking when evidence is split or noisy.
